symptoms: Thoạt đầu, các đốm không rõ nét, màu xanh ô-liu, xuất hiện ở mặt dưới lá, đặc biệt là những lá già gần với mặt đất. Quá trình hình thành của nấm mốc từ màu nâu nhạt ngả sang màu xám có thể diễn ra trên bề mặt lá bị nhiễm bệnh. Khi bệnh tiến triển, các đốm ấy hoại tử dần và có thể xuất hiện ở mặt trên lá. Cuối cùng, những lá bị nhiễm bệnh khô dần và héo rũ. Thân và quả cũng có thể chịu ảnh hưởng với những triệu chứng tương tự như thế. Khi nhiễm bệnh này, cây có thể rụng lá hoàn toàn. Các triệu chứng nêu trên có thể gây ngộ nhận là cây nhiễm bệnh do loài C. malayensis gây ra.
organic_control: Cho đến nay, chúng tôi chưa biết bất kỳ biện pháp sinh học nào có thể đối phó với bệnh hại này. Nếu bạn biết bất kỳ phương pháp nào có thể giảm thiểu khả năng lây nhiễm hay mức độ nghiêm trọng của các triệu chứng của bệnh này, xin vui lòng liên hệ với chúng tôi.
chemical_control: Ở mọi trường hợp, nên xem xét khả năng dung hợp các biện pháp phòng ngừa cùng với các biện pháp kiểm soát sinh học có thể thực hiện được. Phun thuốc diệt nấm vào mặt dưới lá vào buổi chiều tối. Sử dụng các loại thuốc diệt nấm mang tính phòng ngừa như copper oxychloride 0.3%, Mancozeb 0.25% hay Zineb 0.2% vào thời điểm một tháng sau khi gieo trồng và phun tiếp cách hai tuần một lần, tùy thuộc mức độ nghiêm trọng của bệnh. Sử dụng thuốc Carbendazim 50 DF 0.1% với tần suất 15 ngày một lần cũng cho thấy kết quả khống chế bệnh đáng kể.
cause: Các đốm lá nêu trên phát sinh từ loài nấm có tên khoa học là Pseudocercospora abelmoschi. Loài nấm này sinh tồn qua mùa đông trên các tàn dư cây trồng bị nhiễm bệnh trong đất, từ đó lây nhiễm vào rễ và những lá ở tầng thấp của cây đậu bắp. Trên cây ký chủ, bào tử nấm phát tán thứ cấp nhờ gió, mưa, nước tưới và các công cụ làm vườn. Các đốm lá thường xuất hiện trong thời tiết ẩm ướt (thời kỳ đậu bắp trổ hoa) vì loài nấm này phát triển mạnh trong thời tiết nóng ẩm.
prevention: Chỉ sử dụng nguyên liệu hạt giống đã được chứng nhận để gieo và bảo đảm khoảng cách hợp lý giữa các cây khi trồng, sao cho lá có thể khô nhờ thoáng khí. | Giám sát vườn trồng thường xuyên, loại bỏ và tiêu hủy những lá bị nhiễm bệnh một cách phù hợp (đốt sạch chúng là một lựa chọn hợp lý). | Nên quản lý tốt cỏ dại trong vườn. | Tránh cho cây gặp phải những áp lực về điều kiện sống bằng cách tưới nước và bón phân đầy đủ. | Nên tưới cây vào buổi sáng thay vì tưới vào lúc chiều tối và không nên tưới phun từ trên cao cũng như tránh trồng đậu bắp trên các khu vực đất thoát nước kém. | Nên xem xét luân canh đậu bắp với các loài cây trồng không phải là ký chủ của bệnh này.
summary: Các đốm góc cạnh, đen như muội than, xuất hiện ở mặt dưới lá. | Lá khô, héo rũ và bạc màu dần. | Thân và quả cũng bị ảnh hưởng.

title: Leaf spot disease in corn
symptoms: At first, dead leaf spots with a surrounding light-colored halo, which can reach a diameter of 0.5 cm, appear on the leaves. Severely infected plants may have completely yellow leaves. Lesions and mold appear on the seeds, causing the seeds to not germinate or the seedlings to die. In infected plants, leaves have unusual colors along with necrotic areas. Infected seeds may be discolored, with lesions, mold and rot.
organic_control: To date, we are not aware of any biological measures that can deal with this disease. If you know of any methods that can reduce the likelihood of infection or the severity of symptoms of this disease, please contact us.
chemical_control: In all cases, the possibility of integrating preventative measures with possible biological control measures should be considered. Fungicides such as Mancozeb, Chlorothalonil, and Maneb can be used to deal with this disease.
cause: The above symptoms come from a fungus with the scientific name C. lunatus. Fungal infection can occur because spores and sporangia of the fungus are dispersed by wind, rainwater and irrigation water. This harmful fungus can also survive in soil thanks to infected crop residue. This disease often occurs in hot and humid areas and the optimal temperature for fungal growth is 24 -30°C.
prevention: Use disease-free seeds for sowing and choose existing disease-resistant varieties for planting. | Harvest early to reduce the number of mold-infected seeds. | Remove and destroy crop residue after each growing season.
summary: Small spots with halos or areas of dead tissue appear on leaves. | Plants are discolored, seeds are hollow and have lesions.

title: Olive leaf spot disease
symptoms: In late spring, black spots (commonly known as peacock spots) appear on the upper surface of leaves in the lower canopy. These spots can also appear on stems and fruits, but most commonly on the leaf surface. The underside of leaves does not show any obvious symptoms. As the season progresses, black spots develop and can cover a sizable portion of the leaf (0.25 and 1.27 cm in diameter). Gradual yellow halo appear around these spots and extend over the entire leaf. The plant may lose its leaves and in severe cases, the blooms may also be damaged, leading to a significant reduction in crop yield.
organic_control: Spray plant leaves with organic copper compounds such as Bordeaux mixture after fruit harvest in autumn and again in late winter in case of frequent autumn rains.
chemical_control: Where possible always consider using a combination of preventative measures with biological treatments. Spray copper compounds (e.g. copper hydroxide, copper oxychloride, tribasic copper sulfate and copper oxide) on the tree's foliage after fruit harvest in the fall and again in late winter if the environment is too wet.
cause: The disease is caused by the fungus Fusicladium oleagineum, which thrives in low-lying areas or in environments that receive little sunlight or have closed tree canopies. The fungus requires mild to low temperatures and free foliar moisture to germinate and, therefore, it often results in infection during rainy periods in the fall, winter and spring. Fog, dew and high humidity are important factors contributing to the spread of the disease. In contrast, hot and dry conditions in summer cause dormancy and eventual dormancy, which is indicated by a change in the color of the spots, which turn white and crust over. Young leaves are more susceptible to the disease than older leaves. The preferred temperature range is 14–24°C plant susceptibility. For example, excess nitrogen and calcium deficiency are thought to weaken plant defenses.
prevention: Monitor cultivation regularly for symptoms of the disease. | Avoid over-fertilizing with nitrogen and calcium deficiency. | Choose disease-resistant or tolerant varieties, if available in your area.
summary: Dark, black spots on the upper surface of leaves, gradually increasing. | A yellow halo surrounds each spot. | Leaf drop, branch death, and failure to bloom may occur.

title: Orange rust disease in sugarcane
symptoms: The lesions begin to appear as small necrotic spots on the leaves. The spots then develop into brownish-orange lesions measuring up to 4 mm long and 3 mm wide. The lesions are often concentrated at the base of the leaves and appear in clusters. Orange fungal spores are produced on the underside of the leaves. Severely affected leaf tissue will die, leading to the leaf canopy shrinking. In severe cases of infection Critically, necrotic spots also appear on the leaf sheath, causing the entire foliage to turn brown when viewed from a distance.
organic_control: To date, we are not aware of any biological measures that can effectively deal with this disease. If you know of any methods that effectively reduce the impact or severity of disease symptoms, please contact us.
chemical_control: In all cases, the possibility of integrating preventative measures with possible biological control measures should be considered. The use of fungicides can contribute to reducing yield losses. Spraying of Strobilurin fungicide products such as metconazole and propiconazole can be applied in multiple applications at 3 -4 week intervals.
cause: This disease is caused by the fungus Puccinia kuehnii, spread through rust spots where the fungus produces spores that are extremely small, light and able to withstand harsh environmental conditions and easily disperse quickly at close distances as well as spread by wind and water. Spores of this fungus can also survive on crop residue in the soil. The disease is often detected in the summer and early autumn with high humidity and warm environmental conditions. Mature sugarcane plants (more than 6 months old) are more susceptible to the disease. Development and spread of the disease are limited when temperatures exceed 30°C and humidity is between 70 and 90%. Strong winds and continuous overcast weather make the disease worse.
prevention: Choose to plant disease-resistant varieties. | Implement a good hygiene regime for the sugarcane field or garden, such as removing crop residue. | Regularly monitor the sugarcane field and garden to detect early signs of disease.
summary: Brownish orange lesions appear on the plant. | Fungal spores appear on the underside of the leaves. | Lesions also appear on the leaf sheath.

title: Rust disease damages beans
symptoms: Brown patches formed by spores appear on both sides of the leaves as well as on the stem. In dry weather, these spore patches gradually spread. The leaves become deformed and the entire plant grows weakly. However, the harvest yield only decreases slightly.
organic_control: Damage is mainly detected in the late state of the disease. In most cases, treatment is not necessary because the loss of revenue is insignificant.
chemical_control: Fungicides containing Tebuconazole can be used.
cause: The fungus that causes this disease overwinters on fava beans (also known as vetch, millet or English beans), flower beans and euphorbias. From there, it spreads to the bean plant in the spring. In the winter, the fungus switches to a new host plant.
prevention: Remove all alternative host plants in adjacent areas, such as Vicia or Lathyrus species .
summary: Brown patches formed by fungal spores appear on leaves and stems. | Leaves are deformed. | The plant's ability to grow is impaired.

title: Rust disease damages peanuts
symptoms: Peanut rust appears as tiny round orange-brown pimples (rust), usually found on the underside of leaves. These spots are often surrounded by a yellow halo. This condition significantly impairs leaf and plant growth. As the disease progresses, heavily infected leaves become covered with rust-colored pimples on both sides, the leaves turn yellow and look 'rusty', eventually with curled leaves. Reddish-brown (and later black) elongated bumps may also appear on peanuts, stems and leaf stalks. This disease can significantly reduce fruit yield and seed oil quality.
organic_control: Biological agents can contribute to controlling the infection process. Sage and Potentilla erecta extracts protect leaves against fungal growth. Other plant extracts such as flaxseed oil and peanut oil are also effective in reducing disease incidence.
chemical_control: If possible, always consider a combination of preventive measures and biological treatments. Chemical treatments may not be effective in the late stages of disease development. If fungicides are necessary, spray with products containing mancozeb, propiconazole or chlorothalonil (3 g/liter of water). Spraying should be started immediately after the first signs of infection appear and should be repeated later. 15 days.
cause: The peanut rust pathogen exists in crop residues in the soil or in other legumes that serve as alternative host plants. Primary infection comes from spores produced during the reproductive stage of the fungus on the underside of leaves. Secondary infection occurs through wind-dispersed spores. Spots can spread rapidly in environmental conditions favorable for fungal growth, such as warm temperatures (21 to 26°C) and wet, cloudy weather (fog or heavy night dew). They also inhibit shoot and root growth, leading to stunted growth. High levels of phosphate in the soil seem to slow down the development of rust.
prevention: Use seeds from healthy plants or from certified sources. | Choose to plant disease-resistant varieties. | Avoid high humidity between plants by wide planting distances. | Control weeds and naturally occurring plants in and around the field. | Avoid planting alternative host species around the field. | Apply large amounts of phosphate fertilizer to slow the development of rust. | Remove or destroy infected plants and field debris. garden by burning or plowing them. Should rotate crops that are not hosts to this harmful fungus every three to four years.
summary: Rust-colored pimples appear on the underside of the leaf, surrounded by a yellow halo. | Severely infected leaves are covered with pimples on both sides. | Leaves turn yellow and curl.

title: Rust disease in soybeans
symptoms: Symptoms of soybean rust disease typically start appearing on the lower parts of the plant and gradually move upward, primarily affecting young leaves. The first signs appear during the flowering stage, manifesting as small red-brown spots on the underside of leaves, usually along the leaf veins. Over time, these spots increase in size and number, turning brown-red or black. As the disease progresses, the spots are covered by raised fungal structures that can be seen with the naked eye. Some of these structures form clusters and create irregular brown patches, surrounded by a yellow ring. At this stage, the spots appear on both sides of the leaves as well as on leaf stalks and stems. The plant may experience early leaf drop.
organic_control: Use products containing essential oils such as eucalyptus (Corymbia citriodoria) at 1%, lemongrass (Cymbopogon nardus) at 0.5%, and thyme (Thymus vulgaris) at 0.3% to reduce the severity of the disease.
chemical_control: Always consider combining preventive measures with biological treatments where possible. Selecting the right fungicide and applying it at the correct time is crucial for controlling this fungus. Use fungicides containing hexaconazole (2 ml/liter of water) and propiconazole (1 ml/liter of water). It is recommended to use fungicides containing maneb zinc sulfide regularly throughout the growing season.
cause: Soybean rust is a highly contagious disease caused by the fungus Phakopsora pachyrhizi. This fungus does not survive on seeds and requires living plant tissue to grow and complete its life cycle. When there are no soybean plants available for parasitism, this fungus needs alternative host plants to survive. Spores are produced within fungal structures on plants, spreading from one plant to another via wind and can directly penetrate plant cells without going through stomata or wounds on leaf tissues. The disease develops favorably under conditions where leaves remain wet for 6 to 12 hours continuously, temperatures ranging from cool to warm (16 to 28°C), and high humidity (above 75%).
prevention: Choose varieties with good resistance or tolerance to the disease. | Plant early in the season and, if possible, select early-maturing varieties. | Alternatively, plant later in the season to take advantage of drier periods. | Plant crops with wider row spacing to improve air circulation and facilitate drying of foliage. | Regularly monitor crops and remove intermediate host plants. | Manage soil fertility, particularly potassium and phosphorus levels.

title: Rust disease is commonly found in corn
symptoms: Spotting appears on both sides of the leaf and slowly develops into small, brown, slightly raised spots. These spots mainly grow longer and then turn into yellow-brown, powdery, patches scattered on the top and bottom of the leaves. The color may change to black as the plant matures. In contrast to other rust diseases, symptoms do not usually appear on other parts of the plant, such as the stem, However, the stems tend to grow weak, soft and brittle. Young leaf tissue is more susceptible to fungal infection than mature leaves. Plants infected in the early stages may exhibit yellowing and wilting of leaves, leading to high yield loss if upper leaves are affected.
organic_control: To date there are no alternative treatments against Puccinia sorghi. Please contact us in case you know of any methods that can help fight this disease. We look forward to hearing from you.
chemical_control: Where possible, always consider a hybrid solution between preventative measures and biological treatments. Fungicides can be beneficial when used on susceptible varieties. Apply foliar fungicides early in the season if rust is likely to spread rapidly due to weather conditions. Many fungicides are available to control rust. Products containing mancozeb, pyraclostrobin, pyraclostrobin + metconazole, pyraclostrobin + fluxaccoroxadad, azoxystrobin + propiconazole, trifloxystrobin + prothioconazole can be used to control the disease: Spray mancozeb @ 2.5 g/l as soon as the spots appear and repeat at 10-day intervals until flowering.
cause: The disease is caused by the fungus Puccinia sorghi. The fungus overwinters on an alternative host plant (a species of Sorrel) and releases spores in the spring. The spores can be transported over long distances by wind and rain. They initiate the infection process when they land on leaves. Secondary infection from one plant to another can also occur by wind and rain. Disease development is accelerated when relative humidity is high (close to 100%), dew, rain and cool temperatures between 15 and 20°C (may vary by region). In turn, hot, dry weather will slow or hinder fungal growth and disease incidence. This is a problem for fields producing seeds and sweet corn theater.
prevention: Plant locally available disease-resistant varieties. | Plant early to avoid optimal conditions for fungal infection. | Use shorter-season varieties that mature earlier. | Monitor fields regularly for signs of disease, even more frequently during overcast and cloudy weather. | Ensure balanced fertilization along with nitrogen fertilization. | Rotate crops with crops that are not susceptible to disease.
summary:
Lesions begin as leaf spots that develop into small light yellow-brown spots.
Found on both the top and bottom of leaves
The spots turn from dark brown to black later in the season.

title: Rust disease on grapes
symptoms: At first, orange-brown powdery masses can be found on the underside of the leaves. Later, small yellowish-brown lesions appear on both sides of the leaves. As the disease progresses, the orange masses turn dark brown and then almost black and form oblong lesions. When the infection is severe, the entire plant turns yellow or brown, and eventually the leaves fall prematurely. In the following season, the buds develop poorly, causing the entire vine to grow red. This disease can cause poor bud growth, reduce fruit quality and cause harvest losses.
organic_control: Spray a sulfur containing fungicide once on the leaves. Avoid spraying during rainy weather to ensure the fungicide can effectively target the pathogen.
chemical_control: Always consider using a combination of preventative measures combined with biological treatments, if applicable. Use of fungicides containing Bordeaux mixture, aptafol, difolatan, propiconazole, tebuconazole or azoxystrobin has been found to be effective in significantly reducing pathogen occurrence. Grapevine rust symptoms can be controlled by applying 3-4 applications of Baycor (0.1%) every two weeks during the following growing seasons
cause: The above symptoms are caused by the fungus Phakopsora vitis. Fungal spores survive on crop residue and alternative host plants, dispersed by the wind. The rust pathogen develops in spots present on the leaf surface in the lower leaf layer in the form of small fine orange particles. Orange-yellow summer spore masses are produced on the underside of the leaves, with dark necrotic spots on the surface. Temperature levels above 20°C and the Humid weather conditions are favorable factors for the disease to develop. Fungal spores can be easily spread by wind and air currents.
prevention: Use resistant varieties, if available. | Monitor vineyards regularly for symptoms of rust on the underside of leaves. | Collect and burn infected plant parts. | Prune trellises to facilitate air circulation.
summary: Orange-brown powdery masses appear on the underside of leaves. | Premature leaf drop.

title: Southern rust disease
symptoms: Southern rust disease appears as small, reddish-brown spots on the upper surface of older leaves, scattered on the lower surface. These spots are powdery, circular to oval, raised, and dense. In later stages of the disease, they become dense and may also appear on young leaves, leaf sheaths, corn leaves, and plant stems. Yellow chlorotic spots and brown necrotic areas are also present on the leaves. Young leaves are more susceptible to damage than older leaves, making crops planted later more prone to infection. Poor plant health leads to stem rot and breakage, and poor seed quality. The disease's spread potential can cause significant yield losses.
organic_control: Apply water extract of snake plant (Mikania glomerata) to inhibit spore germination. The extract can be prepared by soaking the entire snake plant leaves in distilled water and storing the solution in a refrigerator for 24 hours. Then filter the extract through filter paper, dilute in water until a 5% concentration is achieved, and apply to the leaves.
chemical_control: It is important to consider an integrated approach between preventive measures and biological control methods. The application of fungicides cannot cure already infected parts, so it can only be used preventively to avoid spread among healthy plants. Timely use of fungicides is also crucial, considering factors such as plant age, disease incidence, and weather conditions. Fungicides based on mancozeb, cyproconazole, flutriafol + fluoxastrobin, pyraclostrobin, pyraclostrobin + metconazole, azoxystrobin + propiconazole, trifloxystrobin + prothioconzole can reduce the impact of the disease. For example, treatment could be: Spray mancozeb @ 2.5 g/l immediately when spots appear and repeat every 10 days until flowering.
cause: Southern rust disease is a fungal disease caused by Puccinia polysora, commonly occurring in tropical to subtropical regions during later stages of plant development. The fungus is a facultative parasite, meaning it can only survive on host plants rather than in soil or seeds. Therefore, infection during this season may not necessarily increase the disease incidence in subsequent seasons. Wind-dispersed spores from fields or other areas are the primary source of infection. These spores then spread from one plant to another via wind and water under optimal conditions of temperatures between 27°C and 33°C with high humidity. Early fungal infection during planting can lead to rapid and severe damage.
prevention: Use resistant or tolerant varieties available locally. | Avoid planting corn at the end of the season. | Monitor fields for any signs of disease. | Ensure balanced fertilization to enhance plant health. | Avoid overwatering during post-emergence growth stages.
summary: Small reddish-brown spots with powdery appearance develop densely on the upper surface of older leaves. | Later on, they begin to appear on young leaves and other parts of the plant. | Yellow chlorotic spots and brown necrotic areas are also present on the leaves. | Poor plant health leads to stem rot and breakage, resulting in poor seed quality.

title: Ten bean rust disease
symptoms: Symptoms initially appear as small, round, reddish-brown spots growing between growing white fungal spots, often on the underside of leaves. Pustules may appear in small groups and then merge into larger areas on the leaf blade. Additionally, straight, dark brown areas appear later in the season. Severe infections can affect the upper leaf surface, leading to entire leaf coverage. Leaves become dry, shriveled and fall off. At this stage of the disease, leaf petioles, bark and stems may also be affected. Severe defoliation leads to severe yield loss.
organic_control: Botanical extracts of sage (Salvia officinalis) and Potentilla erecta work well as a protective measure against fungal growth once signs are detected.
chemical_control: Always consider an integrated approach of preventative measures along with biological treatments where available. Chemical treatment may not be feasible if the fungal infection is detected at later stages. If fungicides are required, products containing mancozeb, propiconazole, copper or sulfur compounds can be used as foliar spray solutions (usually 3g/l of water). Start application immediately after the first appearance of the disease and repeat after 15 days.
cause: The pathogen survives in crop residues in the soil or on alternative hosts. Primary fungal infection is caused by spores splashed from the soil onto older leaves at the base of plants. Secondary spread from field to field is by wind. Initiation of fungal infection and dispersal are promoted by warm temperatures (21 to 26°C), wet and cloudy weather combined with periods of dense fog during the night.
prevention: Use seedlings from healthy plants or certified pathogen-free seeds. | Plant tolerant or resistant cultivars if available in your area. | Avoid planting alternative hosts in your growing area. | Remove alternative host plants and weeds from your fields. | Observe and remove infected plant parts. | Be sure to rotate crops with non-host plants every 3 -4 years. |. Plow deeply and remove plant debris by burning or plowing it.
summary: Small, round reddish-brown spots appear on the underside of leaves. | The spots then merge into larger areas and appear above the leaf surface. | Straight, dark brown areas appear later in the season. | The disease can be seen on petioles, bark and stems. | Leaves dry, shrivel and fall, leading to defoliation and reduced yield.

title: Corn stem rot disease
symptoms: The above symptoms can vary depending on environmental conditions and severity of the disease. Infected trees can be easily detected based on abnormal height, pale, stunted and long growth. Infected seeds have lesions, rot and deformity. Infected tree trunks have discolored, moldy, barren or curled bark. Infected leaves are abnormally colored and musty. Infected tips have Lesions, black or brown scabs and rotten corn. All infected plants show signs of falling and premature aging.
organic_control: Spray neem extracts to control the population of disease-carrying insects. Use biological control agents such as antagonistic fungi of the genus Trichoderma spp. to suppress pathogens. Pseudomonas fleurosense bacteria are also effective in controlling stalk rot in corn. Both ingredients can be used as seed treatments as well as soil fertilizers when mixed with 250 kg of manure (FYM).
chemical_control: In all cases, the possibility of integrating preventative measures with possible biological control measures should be considered. Treat the seeds with a 50% solution of mancozeb and 25% carbendazim before sowing.
cause: This disease arises from a fungus that exists in the soil with the scientific name Gibberella fujikuroi. The pathogen is fungal spores spread by wind and rain, penetrating corn cobs through lesions. Plants are infected from the time the seeds germinate until the male flowers emerge, but symptoms only appear clearly in the later stages. This pathogen survives in seeds, infected crop residue left over from the previous crop or on alternative hosts such as Grass. The disease spreads when fungal spores infect the plant through the corn stalks, roots and stalks. Fungal spores penetrate the corn cob mainly through damage caused by insect feeding activities. They can also gradually invade the plant starting from the roots and moving up the stem through the growth process in warm (26 -28°C) and humid weather when the plant enters the flowering stage.
prevention: Use disease-free seeds for planting and use highly resistant corn varieties such as SC 637. | Ensures strong and healthy stalks by minimizing environmental stress. | Limit plant density to 28,000 to 32,000 plants per acre (0.4 hectares) by planting 70 -90 cm between rows and 30 -50 cm giữa các cây để có thể dễ dàng phát hiện các cây có thân bị thối rữa. | Đảm bảo chế độ tưới nước cho cây, khống chế cỏ dại và duy trì mức dưỡng chất thích hợp cho đất. | Giám sát cây trồng một cách cẩn trọng, chú tâm phát hiện những vệt sọc trắng trên bề mặt hạt, tình trạng hóa vàng ở lá non và các triệu chứng héo rũ của cây ở giai đoạn ra hoa và màu nâu đỏ của thân cây bị nhiễm bệnh ở giai đoan ban đầu. | Khống chế sự phát triển của các loài sâu đục thân vì chúng góp phần phân tán mầm bệnh. | Bảo đảm chế độ bón phân hợp lý trong các giai đoạn cuối quá trình tăng trưởng của cây. | Loại bỏ và chôn vùi kỹ những cây bị nhiễm bệnh. | Cày đất để chôn vùi tàn dư cây trồng của vụ mùa bị nhiễm bệnh. | Vệ sinh sạch sẽ kho chứa. | Trước khi cho vào kho, sấy hạt cho đến khi độ ẩm của hạt hạ xuống đến mức 15% hay thấp hơn. | Trữ hạt ở trong điều kiện độ ẩm và nhiệt độ thấp. | Sau After 3 years of growing corn, crop rotation should be applied with legumes or soybeans.
summary: Weak stems. | Small, black fungal structures appear on stems. | Discolored corn. | Plant growth is stunted.

title: Cordana leaf spot on Banana
symptoms: Yellow or light brown, oval or eye-shaped spots that appear near the edges of lower leaves. Over time, the spots spread and the tissue in their central area dies and gradually forms a clear concentric area. These spots can grow longer along the veins as the leaf grows. Some spots can coalesce to form large patches of dead tissue surrounded by yellow tissue. When the leaf margins are infected, the spots can small concentric growths, then the dead tissue forms long, light brown stripes. These stripes sometimes extend to the midrib. Infected leaves can be quite visible because the infected areas are often surrounded by a bright yellow halo.
organic_control: There is no purely biological solution against this disease. Therefore, proper management of banana gardens is very important. In severe cases, organic copper preparations, for example Bordeaux mixture 1%, can be sprayed on infected areas.
chemical_control: If possible, always consider a combination of preventative measures and biological treatments. Because there are a number of different leaf spot diseases in bananas, make sure you are dealing with Cordana leaf spot and not freckle leaf spot or Sigatoka leaf spot. In severe cases, use mancozeb 0.4% or oil-based preparations containing copper oxygen chloride 0.2-0.4%. Recommended. fungicides such as chlorothalonil or mancozeb and root fungicides, such as tebuconazole or propiconazole, be sure to spray the leaves at the top.
cause: These symptoms are caused by the fungus Cordana musae. Also known as Cordana leaf spot, this disease is one of the most important fungal diseases commonly found in bananas, prevalent in most banana-growing areas. The pathogen is spread by spray water and wind, causing the disease to spread to nearby fields. Fungal growth is accelerated in hot, humid conditions and frequent rains. Leaf damage caused by this disease significantly reduces the photosynthetic area and crop productivity.
prevention: Use resistant varieties if available in your area (some varieties are commercially available). | Create adequate spacing between plants to avoid shading and leaf contact. | Make sure new plantings are away from infected plantings. | Avoid sprinkler irrigation to minimize relative humidity and use drip irrigation instead. | Apply a balanced fertilization regimen and especially avoid applying too much nitrogen. | Remove contaminated leaves diseases and burn them. |. Practice field hygiene by removing dry leaves from plants.
summary: Oval yellow or light brown spots appear near the edges of lower leaves. | Dead tissue forms long light brown stripes. | Infected areas have a yellow halo.

title: Bugs nibble on young fruit on Bananas
symptoms: Adult beetles feed on a variety of weeds, as well as unopened young leaves, stems and roots of banana plants. They also feed on young fruit, causing scars and spots on the skin, disfiguring the fruit and making it unmarketable. Most scars appear near the stem of the fruit, reflecting the fact that the beetle chooses the parts of the plant that are best protected to feed on (e.g., underneath the banana flower sheath). Most scars are oval in shape. and can be confused with scars caused by the fruit scarring wasp Melipona amalthea. The damage becomes worse if the damaged tissues are invaded by opportunistic pathogens that feed on young roots and burrow into old roots to feed on root tissue. This pest is often found in abundance during the rainy season.
organic_control: Up to now, there are almost no suitable biological treatments to prevent this pest. The best preventive measure to avoid the proliferation of this bug is to have a reasonable weeding plan.
chemical_control: Where possible, always consider a combination of preventative measures and biological treatments. Chemical control should not normally be used; careful weeding can reduce the population of beetles enough to replace the use of insecticides. Spray insecticides on a rotational basis depending on the pest population. However, unless the beetles are causing serious economic damage, their use should be avoided. pesticides.
cause: The damage mentioned above comes from a beetle with the scientific name Colaspis hypochlora. This beetle often causes scars on bananas. Adult beetles have brown front wings with very characteristic rows of small parallel dots. They fly very well. Females lay light lemon yellow eggs, scattered singly or in clusters, with the number ranging from 5 to 45 eggs. Egg laying takes place in cavities that are gnawed by beetles in leaf sheaths near leaf tips or in natural depressions. After 7 to 9 days, the newly hatched larvae begin to eat young plant roots or make burrows into the soft epidermal tissues of old roots to eat them. The larvae have a white, slender and hairy body, and the pupa has an eel skin color, turning darker when mature.
prevention: Maintain careful weed control in plantings, as this beetle thrives on weeds. | Avoid planting bananas near drainage channels. | Remove below-ground pseudostems and prune rhizomes where larvae mature. | Remove damaged plant parts from the planting area. | Rake the soil surface to expose pupae to the ground as food for their natural enemies.
summary: Severe spots or scars on pods and young leaves. | Scars are oval in shape. | Roots damaged by larval feeding.

title: Banana beetles damage bananas
symptoms: Damage caused by larvae. Symptoms initially appear as leaf scratching damage on the leaves as the larvae feed on the chlorophyll content of the leaf tissue. Over time, these leaves show a window-like gnawing pattern, with large, light brown translucent areas. This gnawing damage leads to defoliation in severe cases.
organic_control: Indian evergreen seed extract can help control infestations in the early stages of larval development. Therefore, a 5% solution of this seed extract should be mixed with 1 liter of water.
chemical_control: Where possible, always consider a combination of preventative measures and biological remediation measures. If pesticides are needed, products containing chlorpyrifos can be used for foliar spraying. Be cautious of chemicals listed as having adverse health effects on humans as well as mammals, bees, fish and birds.
cause: The banana beetle is a moth. Therefore, the adults can only be detected late in the evening and during the night. The forelimbs of the sturdy adults are gray with black spots and the hind wings are pink. The larvae are black with a brown head and long brown hairs all over the body.
prevention: Inspect crops or fields for any signs of pests. | Hand-destroy egg masses, larvae, plants, or infected plant parts in the field to stop the progress of the infestation. | Attract adults with light traps for surveillance purposes or mass traps to kill them. | For mass traps, use fire torches to attract immature insects. | Remove infected plants and destroy them with fire.
summary: Scratch damage appears as light brown leaf tissue without chlorophyll. | Rodent damage appears on leaves that open like windows. | In severe cases, gnawing damage leads to leaf loss.

title: Black thrips damage grapes
symptoms: Thrips suck sap from the leaves of the host plant, causing leaf drop and leaf wrinkling. Silvery patches appearing on the leaves are traces of thrips using their mouthparts to puncture leaves to suck sap. In thrips feeding areas, the fruit turns gray. In plants heavily infested with thrips, the fruit looks ugly and does not develop normally.
organic_control: Introduce natural enemies of this pest to the vineyard, such as the predatory stink bug Geocoris ochropterus and the red spider Metaseiulus occidentalis (predatory beetles). Predatory beetles, blue-veined beetles, pirate beetles and other Phytoseiid beetles can help control this sap-sucking thrips.
chemical_control: Always consider using a combination of preventative measures combined with biological treatments, if applicable. Insecticides are unlikely to control thrips effectively, in part due to their mobility, feeding habits, egg protection and pupal stages (developmental stages in the insect's life cycle with morphological changes occurring from the larval stage to the adult stage). Pesticides currently used in some areas of the world are dimethoate and bifenthrin. Spinosad-based products are considered organic control tools for this pest. At all times, follow your local pest management regulations.
cause: The damage mentioned above is caused by adult thrips and their larvae (young thrips) feeding by sucking plant sap. Thrips hatch from eggs and then develop by actively feeding during two larval stages. When mature, adult female thrips are about 1.4-1.5 cm long while male thrips are about 1.3 cm long. They are dark to dark brown in color. Larvae hatch from the eggs, and begin to feed on the larva. like immediately, often feeding in groups. Newly developed beetles are slightly smaller and lighter in color. Thrips feed on the underside of leaves and, when heavily infested, the upper leaf surfaces are also attacked, especially in cooler months. In warmer weather, the thrips life cycle from egg to adult can be completed in as little as 2 weeks.
prevention: Do not plant plants that are susceptible to this pest. | Monitor the garden's bug population regularly and periodically and control weeds that are alternative host plants for thrips around the garden. | Plant plants that are well adapted to the conditions at the planting site. | Apply appropriate plant care measures to help plants maintain their vitality and increase their resistance to damage caused by thrips. | Water plants well and avoid fertilizing Excess nitrogen fertilizer is a factor that can promote the growth of thrips populations.
summary: Silver patches appear on leaves and fruits. | Small shiny gray and black spots (insect droppings) appear on leaves.

title: Ferruginous thrips damage bananas
symptoms: Infestation can occur at any stage of growth and can be seen on leaves, sheaths and fruit. Adults and larvae often settle behind the base of leaf sheaths. Pupae live in colonies and feed by sucking plant sap with their mouthparts. Initial symptoms appear as watery areas on the fruit. Over time, these areas give the fruit a characteristic rusty appearance, with dark red skin turning brown. Usually, damage is only evident on the skin, but if the tree is heavily infected, the entire fruit may show signs of damage. On more mature fruit, cracks may sometimes be seen. The infected fruit chambers in the early stages of development often suffer the most damage.
organic_control: Parasitic insects from the chrysopidae family and ladybugs can be used to control this pest. Some ants can be equally effective, attacking pupae in the soil. If you are not sure that the seedling comes from a healthy source, hot water treatment can help reduce the risk of infection.
chemical_control: If possible, always consider a combination of preventative measures and biological treatment measures. If pesticides are needed, they should be applied to the soil to kill pupae, as well as to plants and fruit to kill adults. Such an approach may be the only possibility to avoid reinfestation.
cause: The above symptoms are mainly caused by the thrips Chaetanaphothrips Signipennis, but other species may also be involved (such as Helionothrips kadaliphilus). This pest is usually transmitted by infected plant parts or, to a lesser extent, directly by flying from plant to plant. Adult beetles are slender, brownish yellow, about 1.3 mm in size and have narrowly fringed wings with fringes. two dark areas on the front wings. The female lays small eggs (not visible to the naked eye) under the leaf sheath and where the fruit touches the plant. After about 7 days, the wingless, cream-colored larvae hatch. They then move to the ground, enter the soil where the plant is planted, and after another 7-10 days, a new generation of thrips can form every year when the weather is hot and humid, their population can peak.
prevention: Use healthy plants from certified sources. | Remove stray plants and avoid planting host plants around main crop fields. | Visit fields and inspect crops periodically for any signs of pests. | Cover fruit chambers from the start to protect plants. | Remove infested plants and destroy them by burning. | Eliminate abandoned plantings as they can act as sources of pest spread.
summary: Cohorts of adults and nymphs can be found on leaves, stems, and fruit. | Bite damage is manifested as watery areas on the fruit. | The spots turn into rough, rusty spots that can change from dark red to dark brown and cover the entire skin. | Mature fruit may also have cracks or burst open.

title: Thrips damage peppers
symptoms: Both nymphs and adults feed on the undersides of young leaves. They scrape and puncture the tissue and suck up the oozing fluid. Infected leaves develop silvery light brown spots and may show signs of deformity (curling). In the worst cases, the leaves are completely deformed and the plant then loses its leaves prematurely. Inflorescence biting shows streaks on the petals and can lead to drying and falling. Scattered, spotted and deformed fruit will reduce prices. Although the infestation occurs throughout the year, it peaks during the drier months and when there is too much nitrogen in the soil.
organic_control: Various biological control agents such as small pirate aphids of the genus Orius, and phytoseiid mites such as Neoseiulus cucumeris and Amblyseius swirskii have been reported to effectively control thrips in pomegranates. Predatory mites such as Euseius sojaensis, E.hibisci and E. tularensis have also been used effectively to control populations on alternative host crops such as pepper and grapes. Spread diatomaceous earth (diatomaceous earth) around the base of the plant and leaves to dehydrate thrips and their larvae (in the evening). Apply neem oil, spinetoram or spinosad on both sides of the leaves and around the base of the plant.
chemical_control: Where possible, always consider a combination of preventative measures and biological treatments. Foliar sprays containing malathion are recommended for thrips control. Use of other insecticides is also effective in reducing the population of S. dorsalis. For example, the use of abamectin, methomyl and dimethoate is known to be effective against thrips in general.
cause: Symptoms are caused by two species of thrips, Scirtothrips dorsalis and Rhiphiphorothrips cruentatus. Adult Scirtothrips dorsalis are straw-colored. Females lay about 50 bean-shaped gray-white eggs, usually inside leaves and young shoots. As the population increases, they will also choose the surface of mature leaf blades. Incubation period is 3-8 days. Newly hatched nymphs are small with red bodies. which then turn yellow-brown. Nymphs enter metamorphosis and then complete their development in loose soil or leaf litter at the base of their host plant. The adult R. cruentatus is a small, slender, soft-bodied insect with dark brown, fringed wings with yellow wings 1.4 mm long.
prevention: Choose resistant varieties, if available. | Use sticky traps to monitor thrips populations. | Another approach is to pluck leaves from infected plants and gently tap them on a piece of white paper. | Remove heavily infected plants from the field. | Water the soil well and avoid over-fertilizing with nitrogen fertilizers. | Avoid overuse of pesticides to preserve beneficial insect populations. | Avoid planting alternative host plants in the surrounding environment. | Remove weeds in and around fields. |. Windbreaks can protect fields from long-distance damage. |. Plow the soil to bring thrips to the surface, exposing them to the sun.
summary: Leaf curling. | Trees lose leaves early. | Flowers and fruits are also affected. | Small and slender insects with dark brown bodies and yellow wings.

title: Fruit Fly Damage Papaya
symptoms: Female flies lay their eggs in young or very young fruits. The punctured skin oozes sap whose color contrasts clearly with the dark green skin. The fly larvae hatch, burrow through the fruit flesh to reach the seed chamber and feed on the developing seeds. The exit holes for the burrows appear clearly on the surface of the fruit. The extensive burrowing process causes the fruit flesh to rot, appearing as brown and sometimes black lesions as the rot progresses. Then, the fruit emits a foul odor, sometimes secreting a liquid-like substance. The fruit peel turns yellow and may peel or become pitted. The fruit may ripen and fall off prematurely.
organic_control: The parasitic wasp Doryctobracon toxotrypanae has the ability to control the population of this harmful fly.
chemical_control: Where possible, always consider a combination of preventative measures and biological treatments. There appears to be no effective insecticide against this fly. Traps containing an insecticide (e.g. malathion or deltamethrin) combined with a specific bait (for male or female flies) are being tested. Fruit can be treated with pesticide fumigation to kill the fly. this.
cause: The above symptoms arise from a fly species with the scientific name Toxotrypana curvicauda. The fly lays eggs in young green papayas. Adult flies are often confused with wasps because of their similar size, color and behavior. The fly has a yellow body with symmetrical black markings on the thorax. The female fly has a long and narrow abdomen with an ovipositor that curves and extends beyond the length of the body. The larvae are white, slender, about 13-15 mm long. The fruit can be attacked by many larvae at the same time and symptoms of damage appear after harvest. Damage to the fruit reaches its peak after the end of the rainy season.
prevention: Monitor damage or place sex hormone traps to monitor fly populations. | Use paper bags to cover fruit to discourage flies from laying eggs on the fruit. | Remove fallen and prematurely ripened fruit from the garden. | Collect fruit infested with fruit flies and destroy them. | Use plants as traps around the garden to attract flies. | Harvest earlier to avoid the worst damage. | Store fruit at 13 to 16°C. |. Plow the soil around the plant to kill adult flies developing in the soil before they appear on the plant.
summary: The fruit turns yellow and falls off early.

title: Leaf rollers damage eggplant
symptoms: The larvae of this pest only cause damage to leaves. The initial symptoms are infested leaves curling up vertically. The worms feed on the inner tissues of these leaves. Damage occurs most in the upper parts of the plant. Curled leaves may turn brown, wilt and dry out. As the infestation worsens, entire parts of the plant turn brown and the plant loses its leaves. This can lead to crop losses. severe damage if measures are not taken to control the population. However, this pest is rarely a major threat to crop growth and yield.
organic_control: Biological control can be achieved by using parasitic wasps, such as Cotesia spp., to reduce pest infestation levels. Predatory insects such as mantises, ladybugs or beetles also help control the population of these pests. Nematodes such as Steinernema spp. can also control pest populations.
chemical_control: In all cases, consideration should be given to the possibility of incorporating applicable prevention and countermeasures. If pesticides are required, spray products containing malathion to reduce leafroller populations.
cause: The butterflies of this caterpillar are medium-sized, light brown to olive green in color with a three-sided dark patch on the outer part of the front wings. Their hind wings are translucent white. Female butterflies lay clusters of about 8-22 eggs on the upper surface of leaves, usually on young leaves. After about 3-5 days, the eggs hatch into larvae that are purple-brown, plump, have cream or yellow concave bumps, and have many long hairs. The larva (worm) stage lasts about 4 weeks, then the worm turns into a pupa inside the rolled leaf. After 7 -10 days, the pupa turns into a new generation of adult butterflies. Each year there are about 3 -4 generations of butterflies like that, depending on weather conditions.
prevention: Should be planted late in the season. | Plant healthy seedlings and have a proper fertilization plan. | Check the garden and plants regularly for any signs of disease or pests. | Use your hands to catch worms and remove infected leaves. | Remove and destroy infested leaves by burning them. | Do not use large-scale pesticides to avoid harming the natural enemies of this pest.
summary: The larvae (worms) eat soft young leaves, causing them to tear and become ragged. | Young leaves curl up along the length of the leaf. | The rolled leaves turn brown and eventually dry. | In severe infestation, the entire plant turns brown and loses its leaves.
